title: Make every task keyboard complete
principle: Every pointer-completable task must be possible with keyboard alone using predictable order, activation, dismissal, and escape behavior.
rationale: Keyboard access serves many motor, vision, power, and assistive-technology users and exposes semantic defects early.
applies_when: Any interactive element or flow exists.
exceptions: A path-dependent drawing action may require an accessible alternative that achieves the same outcome.
implementation: Use semantic controls and established composite-widget key patterns.
Include skip or bypass mechanisms for repeated regions.
verification: Disconnect the pointer and complete primary and recovery tasks.
There are no traps, unreachable actions, or unexplained key behaviors.
accessibility: Visible focus, logical order, and documented widget patterns are required.
responsive: Keyboard paths work when responsive navigation or dialogs replace desktop structures.
performance: Delayed hydration must not leave controls appearing interactive but inert.
browser_notes: Test representative browser and screen-reader pairs, not only synthetic key events.

title: Expose accurate name, role, value, and state
principle: Interactive and meaningful elements expose an accurate accessible name, semantic role, current value or state, and relationships that update with the UI.
rationale: Visual styling cannot communicate control identity or state to non-visual users, and stale ARIA can be more misleading than missing ARIA.
applies_when: A user can operate, inspect, or be notified by an element.
exceptions: Pure decoration is removed from the accessibility tree.
implementation: Prefer native semantics, then add only supported ARIA needed by the pattern.
Keep visible and accessible labels aligned.
verification: Inspect the accessibility tree in every state.
A screen-reader user can identify purpose, state, and consequence before activation.
accessibility: Do not use ARIA to repair behavior that the chosen role does not implement.
responsive: Responsive variants retain equivalent semantics and labels.
performance: Semantic HTML usually reduces scripting and has negligible cost.
browser_notes: Verify the accessibility API mapping in representative browser and assistive-technology combinations.

title: Verify contrast and focus in context
principle: Test actual foreground, background, state, overlay, and focus-indicator combinations rather than approving palette swatches in isolation.
rationale: Opacity, imagery, gradients, disabled styling, and adjacent colors can invalidate nominally accessible tokens in real components.
applies_when: Any text, meaningful graphic, control boundary, state, or focus indication is styled.
exceptions: Incidental decoration that carries no meaning has no contrast requirement but cannot obscure required content.
implementation: Automate known pairs and manually inspect contextual combinations.
Use a focus indicator that remains visible against surrounding states.
verification: Contrast checks pass in every theme and state.
Focus is easy to locate during continuous keyboard traversal.
accessibility: Include non-text contrast, focus appearance, and color-independent cues.
responsive: Indicators are not clipped by overflow or hidden under sticky content.
performance: No material cost; avoid image-based focus effects.
browser_notes: Test forced-colors and native form-control appearance.

title: Announce status without disrupting work
principle: Communicate asynchronous progress, success, and failure through persistent or appropriately live status without unexpected focus movement.
rationale: Visual-only updates are missed non-visually, while aggressive announcements and focus stealing interrupt typing and can repeat stale information.
applies_when: Content changes without a full navigation or direct focus transition.
exceptions: A blocking modal error may require focus when immediate action is necessary and expected.
implementation: Choose status or alert urgency based on consequence.
Keep important results discoverable after the announcement ends.
verification: A screen reader announces each meaningful update once.
Rapid updates are coalesced and do not interrupt current input.
accessibility: Live regions exist before updates when required and messages are concise.
responsive: Status remains visible and does not cover controls on small screens.
performance: Throttle noisy progress updates and avoid unnecessary renders.
browser_notes: Live-region behavior varies; test supported browser and screen-reader pairs.

title: Set and enforce user-centered performance budgets
principle: Define budgets for loading, responsiveness, visual stability, JavaScript, media, and requests, then verify with both lab and field evidence.
rationale: Performance degrades incrementally unless teams have enforceable constraints tied to real user experience.
applies_when: Building, revising, or releasing any production page.
exceptions: Internal prototypes may defer field measurement but still require basic lab checks and no catastrophic regressions.
implementation: Measure representative devices, networks, cache states, and interactions.
verification: CI or release review fails unexplained budget regressions.
Field percentiles are reviewed by route and relevant user segment.
accessibility: Include assistive-technology-compatible behavior under slow loading; performance work cannot remove semantics.
responsive: Measure mobile and constrained layouts with realistic content.
performance: Track LCP, INP, CLS, transfer, execution, and route-specific task latency.
browser_notes: Field metrics and lab tooling differ by browser; interpret coverage explicitly.

title: Ship the least JavaScript that preserves the experience
principle: Use HTML and CSS for document and presentation behavior, isolate interactivity, and defer non-critical client code.
rationale: JavaScript consumes download, parse, execution, memory, and hydration time and can leave visible controls inert on constrained devices.
applies_when: Choosing component boundaries, dependencies, hydration strategy, or client-side effects.
exceptions: Rich local interactions may justify client code after profiling and scope isolation.
implementation: Prefer server or static rendering for non-interactive content.
Code-split optional features and remove duplicate libraries.
verification: Audit route bundles and long tasks.
Primary content and basic navigation remain useful before optional code executes.
accessibility: Progressive semantics and focus behavior survive loading or script failure.
responsive: Do not send desktop-only interactive payloads to narrow contexts without need.
performance: Measure transferred, parsed, and executed code rather than bundle gzip size alone.
browser_notes: Transpile only to the declared baseline and use differential strategies where supported.

title: Make primary content semantically discoverable
principle: Render meaningful titles, headings, text, links, and structured page relationships in semantic HTML available without user gestures.
rationale: Discoverability and accessibility share a dependency on clear document structure and real links rather than opaque client-only interactions.
applies_when: Public content should be indexed, shared, or understood outside the running application.
exceptions: Authenticated or intentionally private application data should not be exposed for indexing.
implementation: Use unique page titles, canonical destinations, semantic headings, descriptive internal links, and appropriate server rendering.
Ensure hidden tabs are not the only location of critical public content.
verification: Inspect rendered HTML and a script-limited crawl.
Each indexable route has a unique purpose and canonical URL.
accessibility: Semantic structure and descriptive links benefit navigation by assistive technology.
responsive: Mobile navigation retains crawlable links and meaningful labels.
performance: Fast stable rendering supports both users and crawling efficiency.
browser_notes: Use platform navigation primitives so URLs and history behave consistently.

title: Keep metadata truthful, unique, and route-specific
principle: Generate titles, descriptions, canonicals, language, social previews, and structured data from the actual route content and publication state.
rationale: Boilerplate or inaccurate metadata creates duplicate results, broken previews, and trust-eroding promises that the page does not fulfill.
applies_when: A public route can appear in search, link previews, feeds, or structured results.
exceptions: Non-indexable routes still need a useful document title and explicit indexing policy.
implementation: Create a route metadata contract with safe fallbacks.
Validate structured data against visible content and update it on status changes.
verification: Crawl a route sample for duplicates, missing values, invalid canonicals, and preview images.
Structured data never asserts unavailable or invisible facts.
accessibility: The document title identifies page and context for assistive-technology navigation.
responsive: Preview images use safe zones and legible crops across consumers.
performance: Metadata is server-rendered and does not wait for unnecessary client requests.
browser_notes: History navigation updates the document title and route metadata consistently.
